verbatim_text: And I'll discuss a few of them here. The first one is about human and LLM collaborations. So, yes, we have a hard problem, and yes, a bunch of them get these incorrect traces. We have online traces, all of them ending up with zero terminal rewards. But a human can look at these traces and find some key piece of exploration that's missing, so maybe some missing glimmer, and through collaborations with the LLM can get to regions of the search space which were previously unexplored. And this is pretty common in practice. We do see human and LLM collaborate to solve problems, open problems, and math even. And now we can also replace, in some cases, this human with another LLM, which we'll call as a verifier. And the reason we can do this is that modern day LLMs come with very strong verification generation gaps. In many cases, it's easier for them to verify traces and find some calculation error, some incorrect lemma or some operation that was incorrect. And that's something that can be used to improve future exploration. Now, the third choice here is actually looking at logs of agent scaffold. So whenever we deploy an LLM in practice, we just don't deploy this model. We have a bunch of scaffolds and harnesses built on top of it.
